notes.note: Cicero approves the killing of the impostor "Marius" (Herophilus/Amatius), put to death by Antony; the pseudo-Marius is not the historical C. Marius and has no registry entity.

The impostor claimed descent not only from Marius but from the great orator L. Licinius Crassus (cos. 95); Cicero, an admirer of Crassus, says he is "sorry" for the abuse of that illustrious name. The grandson here is the pseudo-Marius himself, who has no registry entity.

Junia Tertia, half-sister of Brutus and wife of Cassius, the most active of the conspirators' womenfolk in carrying their correspondence (possibly her sister Junia Secunda, wife of Lepidus, is meant).

notes.anchor: one his brother had sent to him
notes.note: The letter Paulus received was from his brother; the kinship term is left unbound, but Paulus's brother was M. Aemilius Lepidus, the magister equitum. Its closing lines warned that a plot was being laid against the writer.
Cleopatra VII of Egypt, who had been lodged in Caesar's gardens across the Tiber, slipped out of Rome after the assassination; no registry entity for Cleopatra exists, so the surface is left unbound.
An outstanding diplomatic matter concerning the city of Byzantium that Atticus was handling on Cicero's behalf, to be pursued through the agent Pelops.
Pelops of Byzantium, Cicero's local intermediary in the affairs of his clients there; Cicero asks Atticus to call him in.
